questions.question: Explain the ISO 25010 quality model. List and briefly describe the eight quality characteristics defined in this model.
keyPoints: ISO 25010 replaces ISO 9126 and defines software product quality model
Eight characteristics: Functional Suitability, Performance Efficiency, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability
Each characteristic has sub-characteristics (e.g., Usability includes Appropriateness, Recognizability, Learnability, Operability, User Error Protection, User Interface Aesthetics, Accessibility)
Used for evaluating both internal and external quality of software products

questions.question: Define and differentiate between the following software product metrics: (a) Lines of Code (LOC) (b) Coupling (c) Cohesion (d) Depth of Inheritance Tree (DIT)
keyPoints: LOC measures size of software; used for productivity estimation but has limitations across languages
Coupling measures dependency between modules; low coupling is desirable (better maintainability)
Cohesion measures how related elements are within a module; high cohesion is desirable
DIT measures maximum length of path from root node to leaf class in inheritance hierarchy

questions.question: (a) Explain the concept of internal and external software quality metrics. Give two examples of each. (b) Discuss how Cyclomatic Complexity is calculated and its significance in measuring software quality. (c) What is Response for a Class (RFC) metric? How is it measured and why is it important?
keyPoints: Internal metrics (e.g., LOC, Cyclomatic Complexity, Coupling) measured from source code; External metrics (e.g., MTTF, defect density, MTBF) measured during execution
Cyclomatic Complexity V(G) = E - N + 2P (edges - nodes + 2*connected components); values >10 indicate complex code requiring testing
RFC = number of methods that can be invoked in response to a message; high RFC indicates complex class requiring thorough testing
Quality models guide metric selection; metrics help quantify quality attributes for improvement
Flow graph: 4 decision nodes + 1 start + 1 end; V(G) = E - N + 2 = 10 - 8 + 2 = 4, or simply number of regions + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4
Defect Density = 250 / 50 = 5 defects per thousand LOC (or 5 KLOC); acceptable range is typically 1-10 per KLOC depending on project criticality

Practical applications: (1) Test effort estimation using size metrics, (2) Risk assessment using complexity metrics, (3) Maintenance prediction using coupling/cohesion metrics, (4) Process improvement through defect tracking
Metrics guide decision-making, identify refactoring needs, predict maintenance costs, and ensure quality standards are met
